How would you grow by 10X your Instagram following? Like, how would you literally 10X the number of followers on your Instagram account? Option one, would you use the perfect hashtags every time you post? Or option two, would you post stories every day showing your followers your personal life? Or option three, would you post content every day and don't ever offend anyone? Which option do you choose? So put that in the chat. Which one? One, two, or three? Drop that into the chat. Well, what if I was to tell you that the answer is actually none of the above? It's option four. I tried all of them and they don't work. But what really works is called the Instagram Fire Formula. And I'm going to show you how you can 10X your Instagram account in a time period that is way shorter than you think. I run an online landscape photography training business and I teach people how to take beautiful landscape photos. I make master classes that teach a new method that helps photographers take their own stunning photos with the camera that they already own. And my wife, Leah and I, travel all around the world using click funnels for our online business and we make master classes. And she's my videographer when we travel and I sell the master classes that we create using webinars that I would broadcast from home. But to sell anything online, there's always one thing that you always must have. And if you don't have it, it's a very big problem and you're not going to make any money at all. And that one thing is traffic. If you don't have traffic, then you aren't going to be making any money. And what really is traffic? Traffic is another way of saying people. You need people to visit your website. You need people to watch your webinars and register for your challenges. You need people to watch your social media feeds. So two years ago, I wanted more people to watch my webinars. And at that time, I was broadcasting all my live webinars from home. And then I got this idea. What if I could broadcast a live webinar from a really cool wilderness location? Because if we can broadcast live from a really cool location, then more people would be interested in watching that live webinar because of the curiosity factor. So using a Starlink satellite dish and solar panels, we hosted the world's first live webinar literally 15 feet from the edge of the Grand Canyon. And as a result, we got a ton of new traffic. And that webinar was so successful that we did it again this time from the Rocky Mountains. And that was successful too. So when something is working, what should you do? More of it. Do it again. So we did it again this time from New York City, from a hotel room. But we were able to say it live from New York City. And then we did it again live from the sand dunes of Death Valley. And we did it again from the most beautiful lake in the world in Banff National Park. And then we did it again from the frozen surface of a high altitude glacial lake in the Rocky Mountains. And we took all of those live webinars and automated them so that they could run seven days a week. And this is what is known as an auto webinar, which is when you automate a previously recorded webinar so that it can play 24 hours a day. And an auto webinar is kind of like having a little machine that can make money for you without your daily involvement. So I had all these auto webinars ready to make me money, but that's kind of like having a gas-powered lawnmower. A gas-powered lawnmower will cut a lot of grass. But before it does that, you need to pour gas into the tank. And the same thing is true with an auto webinar or a live webinar. You need to pour gas into the tank, but the gas for a webinar is called traffic. You need people. So there are three sources of traffic. The first one is paid traffic. In other words, paid ads. And I was doing a lot of that. The second one is organic search traffic. That is a tough nut to crack. It's a beast. It usually takes years to master. The third one is social media traffic. And I decided I was going to focus on getting traffic from my social media accounts. Now a little bit about my Instagram. I had an Instagram account that was just over five years old. And during those five years, I grew it to 50,000 followers, which is very slow progress. But really, if I have to be totally honest with you, in the first year of my business, I actually bought 10,000 followers, which was a huge mistake. So really, I only had 40,000 followers after five years of work. And I tried to grow that account. I tried everything. I tried giving away cameras and having contests. I tried posting regularly. I tried lifestyle stories like, hey, here's my dinner. Here's my family. Here's me getting in the car. All that stuff. I tried regular reels. Nothing was working. And how did that make me feel? When I looked at Russell's account, and I could see that he had 1.3 million followers compared to my little 40,000, and it just made me want to give up. I thought there's just no hope. I realized that I was actually carrying around with me a whole bunch of false beliefs. And it's kind of like having a suitcase, chain to your ankle, and that suitcase is full of weight lifting weights. Now, put a comment in the chat if you recognize any of these false beliefs. My content is not interesting enough. I'm not good looking enough. I'm not young enough. I don't know how to crack the code. Every one but me has figured this out. I'm the only loser out there. Do you know how that feels? I'm seeing a lot of yeses. And that made me believe that I simply don't have what it takes to grow my social media accounts. So how did this make me feel? It made me feel like a total loser. But sometimes experiencing failure is exactly what we need to motivate us to strive and to overcome and to work harder and work smarter, to figure it out and to crack the code. Here's the thing with failure. And I want you to put this in the chat. My failures are not final. Drop that in the chat. And my failures are not my future. So I decided to try yet again to grow my Instagram account. But before I start, there's a really important principle here. If you're going to make new content, then you need to make the type of content that the platforms actually want. So right now, all the big platforms are prioritizing short form video to copy the amazing success of TikTok. And short form video is a video that's in vertical format, like when you hold your phone vertically, that is less than 60 seconds. So I decided to focus on growing my social media followers that would result in getting more traffic that I could direct to my programs and my webinars. So I started out by using a very common structure. And if you want to know more about this structure, I highly recommend that you buy a book called Create by Eric Thane, because it's the authority on this. So here's the structure. You start out with a hook. Hey, do you want to solve this problem? Or hey, do you want to get the result? And then the story. I had the same problem. And I solved it. Now I feel really good. And then part three, here are the three steps that you can take to solve the problem as well. And then you followed up with a call to action. So here are my results after 14 days of regularly posting reels using that structure. I was getting 100 new follows per day. I thought, hey, that's really good. It's progress. But then I did the math and that's 3,000 new follows per month or 36,000 new follows per year or 100,000 new follows after three years. It would take me 39 years to reach Russell's number. I thought there's got to be a faster way. Then I started to think about it as well, even though I love Russell like a brother. I realized that watching even his posts kind of felt like being in school and that there were times when I would, I have to confess, I would scroll past. I felt guilty about it. But it felt like it was work to be trained. So I thought maybe there's something there where I can offer content that doesn't feel like it's work to my viewers. So I went back to first principles on human psychology and I asked myself, okay, what hook would work? Because the first five seconds of a reel is the most important thing. It's the hook. What is the one question that you have been psychologically, subconsciously asking yourself for your entire life? And I realized after a lot of soul searching, here is what I've been asking myself. Do I have what it takes? And I've been trying to prove to myself for my entire life, I have what it takes. And I've been trying to prove it to others too. And I thought, well, if this is true for me, it's probably true for the rest of all of my viewers. Can I tap into that for the hook? And it made me think of one of the most popular genres in television, which is the TV game show, which has been the number three most popular genre since the advent of television in the 1950s. Because the audience is constantly asking themselves, do I have what it takes? Can I answer the question before the contestant on the TV show answers the question? So I thought, what if we can kind of do the same thing with Instagram reels? So I decided to create an in-your-face challenge that would trigger that, do I have what it takes response? And I call this the Instagram fire formula. Do you want to see my results after I started using the Instagram fire formula? So here's what I did. I had four months of posting, and that included two weeks of doing it the old way, and three and a half months of doing it the new way. I posted five reels a week because I just didn't feel I had time to do seven a week. Almost all my video was shot at home in front of a webcam. And in that four months, I made 94 reels, and I started out with 50,000 followers, which really was 40. And here are my results. Average views per reel were 300,000 views. 15 of those 94 reels went viral with over one million views. And my follower account in the first four months went from 50,000 to 1.6 million followers. So for those of you who are online, drop something in the chat and let me know if you want those kind of results. Do I have your attention? Drop that in the chat right now. So do you want to see my results after seven months? At seven months, we hit 2.1 million followers. Do I have your attention now? So would you like to know how I got 2.1 million followers in just seven months? Because I'm going to show you the exact techniques that I used. Organization is everything. You're going to be making a bunch of reels here, so you start with a Google Sheet. And on the left side of that Google Sheet are the video numbers. I numbered my video sequentially, starting with video number one and then video number two. So those are the video numbers on the left column. The next column over is the name, just sort of a name that will trigger your memory so you know what this video is about. And then the next column after that is, has the script been written? Did you write the script for it? Yes or no? And did you film it? Yes or no? Did you edit the video? Yes or no? And what's the date that you scheduled to post this because you want to schedule your post? And then followed by the last column, which is a link that points to a Google Doc and that Google Doc contains your script. So I used one Google Doc per reel. And what I found, there was a sweet spot with around 140 words per reel written on that one Google Doc. And that brings a link to about 45 seconds, which is a sweet spot. You don't want to be creating all of your videos to be 60 seconds long because the algorithm sees that a lot of people bail out before 60 seconds and then your video is not promoted. You create one Google Doc per script and keep the Docs inside one folder that's in your Google Drive. And then of course, number those scripts starting at number one. So here's a Google Doc from our most successful reel. And I will film as I read from the Doc. A lot of people say, oh, keep it spontaneous. Not in the Instagram Fire formula. Every word is scripted because every word counts. And you can still do your filming so it looks very natural. Any photos that I will be using for that reel, I embed into the Google Doc so that I have the graphics and the text in one place. And once you have the script, then it's actually time to do the filming. So this is how I do my filming. This is my home, half of my home studio. I have a large 75-inch monitor that's behind me. And that provides a different background for every video that I film. I use a Sony mirrorless camera as the webcam that sits right above my monitor. I use good lighting and a professional microphone. Those two black things on either side are what is called a gridded softbox. And you need that eggshell grid in order to avoid having light reflections on the monitor that's behind me. And this is the view that the camera actually sees. The camera only sees that big TV monitor. It's kind of like a green screen effect, but it looks very real. And this way, with every single reel, I can change the background so that it always looks like a different reel. People won't think they're watching the same one. Now this has been a big game changer, and it's called the Elgato Promptor. It's a teleprompter that you buy from the company called Elgato. And it will scroll at the same speed that I read. It sits above my computer monitor, and it clips onto the lens of my camera, which is on a tripod that sits on the desk. If you don't want to buy this teleprompter, you can put that Google Doc directly on the monitor of your computer and read from your monitor. The problem with that is that your eyes won't be looking into your webcam. Your eyes will actually be looking down like this. But there's a workaround. And I use an app for this called Descript.com. It's pretty amazing. It will move the location of your eyes. So instead of looking down, you'll be looking up into the camera. I also use Adobe Podcast to enhance the audio. It does an amazing job. Even if you have a super echoey room and a very low quality mic, it'll make you sound like you're in Joe Rogan's studio. And I use Opus.Pro to generate the subtitles. So the rules with the editing. I use a jump cut ending every time. And what I mean by a jump cut ending is that I'm actually cutting the end off maybe one or two words before the end of the video. And this makes the end of the video a little bit of a surprise and ensures that more people watch to the very end. Keep the videos 45 seconds long. And occasionally, I will apply a surprise ending that I call the Scooby Doo ending. Drop a comment in the chat if you would like to know what the Scooby Doo ending is. Because maybe I'm going to tell you. So then I use three different structures to create the scripts. And I alternate between those three structures so that not all the reels are kind of the same thing. And now this is an important one. You want to keep it as visual as possible. So during the edit, I'm putting in red lines, red arrows, underlines, circles. And I have a video editor named Fernando, the best in the world. And he is a genius in doing that. And it makes the viewer lean forward to look into their phone because they think that like there's something they should be seeing here that's unique. They need to figure it out and you get more engagement. So here are the three structures that I use when I write my script. Structure number one is how would you do this thing? Structure number two is there's a problem here. Can you find the problem? And structure three, here's how you can solve this problem. Or here's how I solve this problem. That's the most common structure that I see in Instagram reels today. So here is an example of structure number three, which is I can help you solve a problem. And this reel went viral with 2.5 million views. I'm going to show you the one rule that you need to remember every time you take a photo if you want to take fantastic photos. And the rule is called the one thing. And what it means is that you're going to choose one clear main subject for every photo you take. Look at this picture of this waterfall. There's one thing to look at. Look at this picture of this pier, one main subject or this picture of this bridge, one clear main subject. Make sure your photos always have one clear hero to that story that you're creating through your viewfinder. But there's one exception to the rule. And that's why I'm standing in a burned out forest to show you the exception. And it's called repeating patterns. Whenever you have a consistent repeating pattern that goes from the right over to the left, then you're going to also have a winning photo. And if you love taking winning photos, then subscribe to this channel. I have so much more to give you. So that's structure number three. And with structure number three, I found that it gives you the least amount of followers and engagement out of the three structures, but I still like to mix it in with the other two. So here's structure number two. Can you find the problem? This particular reel got 14.1 million views. This photo contains the biggest mistake that photographers can make. Do you see the mistake? This photo has one major problem, but there is an easy solution that you can use that will make all your other photos look amazing. So if you guessed that this image has way too much sky, you are right. So if you take photos where the sky takes up more than half of the frame are totally boring, even if you have an incredible sunset, because there's no sense of where the photo is taken. There's no place in the photo when there's too much sky. So here's the simple rule you can follow. When you are taking a picture, make sure that the sky takes up a maximum of about one third of the image. And if you really want to create the best photos of your life, then click on the follow button because I have so many more photography hacks to... Did you hear that jump-cut ending? The surprise ending. So make sure that everybody gets to the end of it. And again, 14 million views on that one, asking people to find a problem. You're poking them right in the heart and challenging them. Do you have what it takes? And that's why the hook here is so effective. And then showing all those arrows gets the engagement. Now we get to structure number one, which is the best structure. How would you solve this problem? Do you have what it takes? Can you do this? So it's just like the question that I asked you at the start of this presentation. It's a direct in-your-face challenge to the viewer, and they cannot ignore it. Now the normal structure is to ask a question and provide three options and get them to choose. But occasionally I will apply what I call the Scooby-Doo ending. I use it very sparingly. So the Scooby-Doo ending. Big cartoon, one of the longest lasting cartoons in history. And in Scooby-Doo, the mystery machine rolls into town and they capture the monster. And then there's a big surprise ending, the unveiling, they rip off the hood. And it turns out it's old man Jones from the general store. Huge surprise. So the Scooby-Doo ending is what I used on you at the start of this presentation. Remember I asked, how would you 10X your Instagram account? I gave you three options and the right answer was option four. How did that make you feel? Did you kind of go, oh, well, if you would have told me there was a number four, I might have chosen number four. And maybe if you had a keyboard in front of you, you would type a comment. That's called engagement. So I want to show you my best reel of all time using the Scooby-Doo ending. How would you photograph this tree? Option one, would you place the tree over on the left side of your photo or option two? Would you place the tree on the right side of your photo or option three? Would you place the tree right in the middle of your photo? Which option did you choose? What if I told you that none of those options are right? Because if you were to walk around that tree and look for something more, you would discover this set of tire tracks going through the grass. This is what is called a leading line in photography and it leads your eye from the bottom of the photo up to the top of the photo. Photos with leading lines are always the right answer. And if you love taking pictures, then follow this channel because I have so many more photography tips to give you. Did you like that one? So and it's simple, simple, simple. Place the content, it's like down at a grade seven level. This is not complicated stuff so that you have a wide net to pull in the most number of people. Now here's one of the comments on that particular reel. I don't understand. You gave us three options. If you gave us the fourth one, maybe someone would pick it. Now the old me, I would have been like, oh no, I made someone upset. But the new me says, thank you for that comment. There's 11,000 likes. Then you fathom the engagement that's there. And that's what made it go viral. It's all about the engagement. So let me show you the final results for that reel. How would you photograph this tree? It got 32 million views, 1.3 million likes, 9,000 comments and 220,000 new followers in just one day off that reel. So type in the comments. I want that if you want to see results in your own Instagram following. So what do you do after you have posted a whole bunch of reels? What do you do with it all? Well the right answer is assess. You need to treat this content as a giant free split test and copy what is working and cut what is not working and then take your most viral reels and then turn them into paid ads because they've been proven to work through your audience in social media. So the other question now is how do you monetize this? Well that's the big one. You ultimately want viewers to click on your link and you want to take those viewers from the social media platform over to your website. But the problem is that Instagram and YouTube removed clickable links from reels and shorts. So there's a workaround. And the workaround is that in your reel you ask the viewer to comment with one word and then you use manychat.com that will automatically send those users who comment with that one word a link that will take them to your website. So here's an example of how I monetize to get people to take a free lead magnet, a free book and you only get the free book when you watch a webinar which I'm calling a workshop. How would you photograph this sunset in the Florida Keys? If you were taking a picture of this same sunset with your camera would you, option one, leave your camera in auto mode and hope for the best or option two, start trying to make changes to your camera settings and also hope for the best. Here's the thing. Everyone finds camera settings confusing, but I can help you and I have something free to give you right now. And it's my new book, 10 sunset secrets you must know. This book will simplify camera settings for you so that you can take amazing sunset photos and even better, I'm also giving you a free photography training workshop. I will give you your book when you watch the training workshop. Just type the word book in the comments and I will send you your private link to watch the training workshop and download your book. So drop a comment now with the word book and I will send you your link right away. So does this work? Oh yes, it works. So what is the net result being for our online business? So speaking of awesome, ladies and gentlemen, here's what I'd like you to do. Go ahead and stand up for me and welcome to the... Bo-Hacky is a process that I was doing ever since I first got started. I didn't, we didn't have a name for it. We never called it that. But basically I wasn't like an amazing business person or that creative or anything. So I was just looking at what everybody else was doing to make money when I first got started. I saw people like Armand Moore and I saw what they did. So I just modeled that as close as I could because it was working. The true story is that I was wrestling. I just met my wife. We were engaged and I had to figure out a way to pay the bills. So the logical choice for me was to look online to figure out how to make money. This is 12 years ago. And I started looking around. I started seeing all these people who were super smart making a bunch of money. I was watching these guys create these products and they were selling them. I remember like their sales letters were so good. I bought every single one of them. And I started seeing all their processes. And so I went and I hired this dude from Romania and he built my first idea. It was this little product called Zip Brander. And then I looked at Armand's websites and he always had these little covers. So I hired some make a little cover and then he had a headline. So I wrote a headline and then in his head or you always had a picture of him in a suit and tie. So I went and got a picture of me in a suit and tie. And I just made it look as close to his as possible because like he was the dude who's making a bunch of money. I was watching right. And because I had followed his process, it started making money. Eventually I went from this college kid who wasn't, you know, didn't have a job, not that educated, just trying to wrestle to within a year of graduating from college. I made over a million dollars in sales. And it wasn't for me being a genius of business. It was for me looking at people who were successful in modeling them. That's kind of how I got started. To understand like where a funnel I can live came from, you have to go back in time. I remember I was going to school. I was making money. By my senior year, I made like $250,000 online, like selling stuff. And I remember my mom like being so nervous, like, what's your plan? What's your job? What are you going to do? I'm like, I'm making money online. She's like, I know, but like when that's done, like, what's your plan? Like what's your backup plan? Like, you know, that was the big, the big thing. In fact, I remember back then it wasn't the main people went online to consume stuff, get knowledge, information. Like it was still like the school system, the traditional as a way. Nowadays, 15 years later, you see where most people it's like, if you want to learn something, you don't go to school. It's like, who created the course? Who's the person wrote the book on school? Learn directly at the feet of the master, right? And that's what how we can look at education today, but it wasn't like that back then. It was weird. Like, I remember the first time I saw an ebook, like it didn't make any sense to me. And then first time I tried to sell the book, people messaged me like, like, where's the book? I'm like, it was an ebook. I don't understand what that means. It means you download it and they're like, why did I pay you then? And I'm like, because the words on the page are mine. Like, and people couldn't even grasp it before Amazon had the ebook. Try it's like physical books were the thing and digital stuff. Like it took this learning curve. People understand, like, why would I pay for something that's that's not I can't touch, you know what I mean? I wasn't the first person that tried to build ClickFunnels or a ClickFunnels. In fact, my first one is called click.com.com. I had six programmers spend three years on it and it never saw the light of day. That's when I met Todd Dickerson. He was like, no, I think I can do it. I was like, all right, man. Well, good luck with that. Like, if you really want to spend the next year of your life trying to do that, like, that'd be awesome. And I knew Todd was good. But I know how amazing was, but he literally like took some of the sketches and the diagrams and ideas we had and he locked himself in his bedroom, his closet, wherever he was at. And in like six months, he built the very first version of ClickFunnels. We didn't know what ClickFunnels were going to become. We thought it was going to be something awesome, but we made, we still made most of our money doing the information. When we launched ClickFunnels, initially we launched this thing and it was interesting because the software without the information didn't actually do anything. We launched it and it was like hitting a brick wall. It was like crickets. Like nobody got it. Nobody understood. It was just the weirdest thing. I'm like, do people know what we created for? Like, if I could just explain, like, this is why this is so important. Like it would change everything, but like it just couldn't get traction. About the same time I'd started my very first book, which is Dotcom Secrets. And I was like, I'm going to get this book done because people read the book. It was almost like a manual. Like, here's the strategy and how this thing works. And it was crazy how the book launched. People got the book, they read it and also they're like, oh, that's why I need a funnel. That's why this is so important. I'm like, I know. So we had now like this book, which was like the training manual to help you strategically understand it, then the tool that let you easily do it. And since we had the book and the software together, that's when it started taking off. People read that and they're like, OK, I get it. And then they needed the software to actually execute on it. I wish I could say that this is all strategically designed to be getting, but it wasn't. And what's funny is that the little community we had at the time, it was a Facebook group. We probably had two or three thousand people at this time. They started calling themselves funnel hackers. Like they started using that as a term. Like people come in like, hey, my fellow funnel hackers, I got a question for you. And like they kept saying that I was like, this is cool. Like they've they've got a name for themselves. And they'd heard me and Todd talk about these little hackathons we used to do. Like we used to do these all night hackathons, string it, stuff done. Like, let's do a hackathon. And so we get these little groups in the country, people do like two and three person meet up hackathons. I was like, hey, this is so cool. Like this is happening without us trying to engineer this thing. And they're doing their own little events. Things are happening. And it's like, we can just let them keep doing it. Or like, what if we facilitated that? People just kept asking, kept asking me and finally I was like, all right, let's let's let's go for it. Let's let's do the men. And so I would recall the men, you know, our people are funnel hackers. Like we're going to funnel hack live together. And then that was the name. I was like, that's that's it. It's fun hacking live. Let's just call it that. It was awesome. So I appreciate you guys. I remember my first seminar of the winter I was dreaming about doing seminars. We tried for a bunch of years. Then we gave up a sword brand in John that we would never do another seminar again. But I'm grateful we did and just grateful for you guys for executing and making it, making it all happen. So thank you. And it's amazing. So thanks to you guys. I'm kind of behind the scenes. So I was the technical co-founder when we started ClickFunnels. Russell's the marketing sell side of the company, the coaching and the events. While I'm the guy running the actual software business and running everything behind the scenes. When I started ClickFunnels with Russell was just me and him. So I was working out of the basement basically. So we grew a little bit and we decided to expand and get an actual office. Get out of my wife's care at the house all day every day. Right. So we're going to read quantum physics. That was more user signing up or going down one or the other. Hopefully it's not down. So funnel hacking live started as the ClickFunnels user conference, I guess you would say. But it's really grown to something much bigger now. Big differences between the different types of events that I've been to at least. So you've got user conferences, which tend to be more technical hands on. This is how you can use this software and this industry to accomplish this goal. Those events kind of tend to be boring, usually. You end up seeing a lot of people just networking in the hallway. Personal development in internet marketing conferences are kind of the other side of the equation that we see a lot of like getting excited about methods and stuff. But there's not really like hands on training necessarily. Oftentimes there's people selling courses to learn more and that kind of thing. Whereas I think we've taken kind of the best of both worlds and mesh them together for funnel hacking live where we've taken and we do hands on stuff. We teach you usable real world things that you can do right now with our software and with our products to actually grow and progress your business. I didn't realize how important having a theme would be initially because we didn't have one. It was fun like you live and we're coming to hack funnels together. I remember sitting down and writing the headline for I think the third from hacking live and I remember thinking the concept of like your one funnel way for being rich. I was like, oh, that's so cool. Like when everyone believed that one phone would be rich, I remember writing the headline and looking out for a minute and sitting back and be like, seems so shallow. Like being rich is cool. That's not why most people do it. Maybe initially, but most people wanted it because the impact on my cable. You're one funnel way from changing the world. That's like, oh, that's really cool. But my God, that speaks to people that impact different. Some people it's not. Some people just hate their job. They just want to get free. It's like, hey, you're one funnel way from firing your boss. I'm like, ah, I deleted everything. Just one funnel way. I'm trying to think, trying to think. And then I kept looking at that. I was like, it's different for everyone. Like the result they want is going to be different. But this is the tool that gets in that result. I said, one funnel way. Like that's the theme. That's the concept. And so the last couple of fun life that's been the theme every year, people are like, what's the new theme? I'm like, no, that's the theme. Like this is a recurring thing. Like this process for all of us, you guys, is an up and a down. Like it's always happening. And all of you guys are somewhere in this wave right now, right? Some of you guys are at the top riding it. Some of you guys are at the bottom in a crash. Some of you guys are somewhere in between and always going up and down. But if nothing else of this event, like I wanted you guys to all understand that, like no matter where you are, there's hope. It might not be this funnel. I can't tell you how many funnels flopped. I guarantee I failed at more funnels than anybody else in this room. Because of that, we found out what worked. And the only way to do that is to do it over and over and over again. And it's a lot easier now. I promise you, it's so much easier than what we used to do. And that's why this tool is so important. Because you can do that. You can test. You can try over and over and over again. And so people are trying to think of a theme. It really comes back to like, what's the end result you're trying to get for your customers coming to the event? Like what is the thing that they're looking for? Like what's, you know, and everyone comes for a different purpose to different reasons. And somehow your event is a vehicle to get them there. And so how does it do that? Like that's what you got to figure out. I kind of have a button niche to where if I scroll too much and I don't see blue, I'm like, oh, I need a button. My name is Jake Leslie. I handle all the design for ClickFunnels. I'm Nick Sanders. And I build the funnels. I manage all the process behind making the funnels and coordinate the team, get everything ready. The Funnel Hacking Live Funnel is probably one of our biggest funnels that we make every year. All the pressures on us to perform and to make it awesome. One mistake on a funnel building side or anything could literally cost us millions and millions of dollars. My design concept basically was based off of if I was sitting in Funnel Hacking Live looking at that stage, what would be my perspective of actually seeing that? And so we're trying to kind of grab that energy throughout the page itself. So the user kind of gets that experience before even the event starts. So going from Russell to copy to design to funnel building to testing to implementing that flow makes a huge, huge difference. Because I know that if I have copy and video that I can kind of base my design off of that energy that that's giving me. The big design tips that I really, really abide by is one, keeping on brand, making sure you're staying consistent. Consistency is everything. All my headlines are the same. All the buttons are the exact same color. And there's no other distracting factors. And that's the biggest thing is getting people excited about the event. That's why we start with the Big Ong Cells page. We always put the promotional video at the start to get people super excited looking at the previous year. And so they get excited to read more about what's coming in this year's event. Late nights when you're sitting there working and you know, you just feel like you can't push anymore. This is one funnel away, one funnel away. That rings true in your head to be like, man, if I just make one thing that's going to work, one funnel that's going to actually work. When you go down a sales page, the audience from the top to the very bottom diminishes very, very rapidly. One key thing is a date and the button above the fold. So I can see clearly see the call to action. I can read the label of the copy. I know exactly what I need to do to opt in. As we scroll down the page, you'll consistently see that same button as you go through almost creating a habit to be like, okay, that's where I click. That's where I click. It goes back into basically the strategy of a funnel. What is the goal of a funnel is to guide your customer to your specific goal that you want them to do. Whereas the website is super distracting and your customers have no idea what's going on. Being able to use ClickFunnels is a huge advantage. Events are crazy, crazy stressful to begin with. This is the last thing that you want to worry about. Once the funnel is done, you're good. You can go off and spend months focusing on your event. This will just make money for you. So I'm going to go ahead and do a quick quick quick quick quick. So it's a vending machine online. I think of all the things that we do, I think FunLocking Live is probably the most important, most valuable thing that we produce, we put on. But the problem is this also of everything we sell is by far the hardest thing to sell. You think about software information products, like it's like an impulse buy, right? With this, it's not an impulse buy because someone's got to go and they have to get work off and they have to convince their spouse and have to find babysitters for their kids and they've got to fly to hotels and there's so many more decisions that are made, not just one. And so for us, it's like a constant grind to sell tickets. Every year, how are we going to sell 4,500 tickets? How are we going to sell 600 tickets? How are we going to sell, you know, 2,000 tickets? And it's just always an impossible task that ends up happening. Our first event that I did, I think cost us maybe half a million dollars, if that, to where now, you know, just the food bill is half a million dollars. Enjoy the food because it's not cheap. But we went from registering 600 people my first year to about 4,000 or more this last year. And we've gone from essentially half a million dollars to over 2 million as we've grown the event. We've probably quadrupled our costs, mostly the hotel. So San Diego was selected before I came into the role, but I know that there was a lot of internet marketers down in San Diego and it's an easy flight for us. The location was perfect. It was a beautiful hotel right on the marina there. And the hotel size was perfect. Like we just took over pretty much the whole hotel and event space. It was a fun first event to put on. Then we went to Dallas and Dallas was a location we wanted to try just because it is kind of like in the middle a little bit more and the space to fit 2,500 people too. And we had dinners that we did onsite and so we just needed to make sure we were able to take over plenty of that hotel. And then from Dallas we went to Orlando and the reason why we went to Orlando is because we were growing quite a bit bigger. Our concern was we were going to have to go to a convention center and that's not the look I love. Like when I do an event I want everyone to feel they're somewhere special. If I went to a convention center I'm putting another $200,000 into an event that needs to be made pretty. When you get it right off the bed at the hotel. So my goal with you today is just to give you some really macro level overviews on what we're going to do together this week and give you some context of what's happening behind the scenes because I think it makes your job so much easier. I'm Barry Baumgartner and I am the founder and executive producer at Sage Event Management. We're the event production company behind Fuddle Hacking Live. Almost always for our clients money is almost secondary. They really care about impact. So when I think about live events and how we're designing them, the clients we work with, we're always starting with what kind of impact do we want to make, how are we going to design the coaching program to deliver that, and then how are we going to reverse engineer the live event to deliver the coaching program. One of the things that I really liked about how this event worked is even though we were at this massive property, when you came into this event you started to recognize little touches like, wait a second, ClickFunnels really is here. When you walked in and you start talking to the people that you're interacting with at the front desk, they're wearing ClickFunnels hats, the TVs behind the front desk are showing a loop of previous ClickFunnels events. As you start to walk to your room, there's signage all over the place. That's ClickFunnels branding. From our perspective, community building is one of the most important things you do at a live event. Everywhere they went we wanted them to feel like, yes, we're funnel hackers. We wanted them to be really super psyched to be part of this tribe. And that was a big thing that we did this year. There was a lot invested by Russell and the team in branding and in print. We've never done anything to that scale, which is amazing. That I want to say was $30,000, $40,000 to get all that done, maybe a little more. It's all of a sudden like you're no longer at a Gaylord property, you're actually at the ClickFunnels hotel. Surround yourself with the dreamers, the doers, the believers, and the thinkers. Most of all, surround yourself with those who see greatness within you even when you don't see it yourself, which is what family is all about. So, it's what the community is about is you be able to bring yourself, surround yourself with amazing people who believe in you. One of the things that you guys are best at, we saw this last year, is just extraordinary customer care. Like you care deeply, you're really committed to the brand and to changing lives. And when you come into an event feeling that way, the attendees feel it and it makes a huge difference. What's happening today that's really critical is registration. And this is one of your first touch point moments. The person who you're seeing at registration left behind a family or a dog or kids or clients. They had to get on a plane or get in a car. They had to pack the bag. There's been some sacrifice for them to get here. They may have also incurred delays at the airport, delays with the pickup, delays in checking into their room. By the time they get to you, I promise you, whatever has stood in their way has started to build up and be like, I sure hope this is worth it. I knew, I know this was a mistake. I shouldn't have even have come. This is going to be a whole challenging week. And we have to remind them at registration that they made the right choice. Like I really see registration as right choice moment. Like that kind of energy is welcome to the family. We're so happy you're here. How did you get here? Where did you come from? What made you decide to come? For those of you who are floating during registration, having those conversations can be the difference between standing in a dull line and having an enlivened conversation that reminded you of why this is the right choice. You have to get that excitement because they've gone through a lot of stuff to get to that event. So you've got to get that energy up the second they walk into register. You know, our whole goal and mission while we're at that event is to make it the best experience for every single attendee. And if we're not keeping that energy up, if we're not excited, if we're not loving what we're doing, they sense that and their energy falls. So once you register, these people come in, they have their name badge, they have their swag bag, they now have their shirt. Like they're ready to go. And then we get to our openings. And the opening is where Russell will really kick it off and set the tone for the event with the help of Devon, who's going to take the stage first and get that crowd going. Music This is not about reading cue cards. If you want to get Bob from accounting to come on stage and read a cue card because he's good at the Christmas parties, hey, you have at it. Your event's probably going to suck. I don't mean to sound mean, but that's not what this is about. My job is to be the through line through the entire event from start to finish. So I'm the very first person your audience sees. And I'm probably the very last person they see. Before every speaker, after every speaker, before every break, I'm giving them instructions. And so if I can't build rapport with them, then I might as well not even try. The letters MC normally stand for master of ceremonies. I would say that my real role is more like an ME, which is the master of energy. I want you to think of me kind of like your event coach. It's to be the through line from speaker to speaker to speaker to speaker to speaker to speaker. It's to tell the audience is what you need to do. Here's where you need to be, etc, etc, etc. It's to say, here's how what this speaker is speaking about. Here's how this is important to the entire event. So give yourself a round of applause for being sexy. Right? You didn't tell Bob to do all that. All you told Bob was to do is introduce the speakers. So Bob is going to go up and read the cue cards. Reading cue cards is not room management. Reading cue cards is not feeling the room, feeling what's going on, being able to engage and have fun with the crowd. Reading the cue cards is, give it up for the last speaker. Our next speaker is Mary. And she wrote three books and she's really great. Give it up for Mary. What the hell was that? It was what you told Bob to do. So you can't get mad at Bob for that. Yeah, that's what I would say about Bob from accounting. When you hire people who have the experience, then what you're really paying for are all of the things that you don't know that you don't know. They're hiring for that very first consultation that says, stop right there. Your schedule is off from the jump because you said 9, 10, 11, 12 and schedules don't work like that. But I can laugh. Are you ready to get this thing going? You're hiring somebody to go out there, read the energy in the room, realize that the last speaker was a snooze and that's okay because he or she was talking about something very technical. Cool. Not mad at that. Throw the cue card out the window. Find out the essence of what the speaker is going to speak about and communicate that in such a way that wakes the audience to hell back up. That's what you're paying for. Those are the intangibles. Those are the, oh my gosh, I didn't even know that I needed that. Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes. So like, you get what you pay for. The most important time for the event MC, 60% of my work is done in the first 15 minutes on day number one, the very first time I go on stage. That first 15 minutes sets the tone for everything. Because I will do an exercise with the audience where I'm going to say, welcome to the stage. And what you're going to do is you have to stand up and you have to go crazy and you have to respond the way as if I'm bringing you to the stage and showing me how you would want the crowd to react when you're on stage. And so we'll do that exercise and that starts me training them. So now the expectation is set before the event kicks off that this is how we introduce the speakers. So it's trained. Wow. What did ClickFunnels send us? Let's see. You have to help your audience break their four minute mile. Now, 1954, Roger Bannister broke what everybody thought was impossible. Four minute mile. Like it wasn't it wasn't possible. It wasn't supposed to happen, right? He did it. And then what happened after he did it? People saw it was possible and they're like, dang, he did it. I can do it. Okay. And for me, I had a similar experience. I wrote the date down this morning. So I didn't forget. August 17, 2004 was the day that the two minute mile, the four minute mile was broken for me. I would just got into this business and I was happy. My goal when I got started was a thousand dollars a month. I mean, a thousand dollars a month. Like, that's it. Like, I'll be, I'll be free. Like my wife and I go on dates. We could get a nice car. Like a thousand bucks a month would change our life. Our basement. We're hoping to have some retros very soon, very soon. My email address at the time was moneyforcollegeatjuno.com and this email popped in and it said subject client was something like we did it. I was like, did what? And I click on this and there's the email from John Reese and John at the time I'd heard about this. He was launching a new course called traffic secrets and an email he explained, he said, look guys, we launched traffic secrets earlier today. He sort of broke down. He said at 18 hours, we had sold a thousand copies at a thousand dollars. So it means we made a million dollars in just 18 hours. I remember sitting there, like everything around me just got quiet. I sat back in my chair and I was like, he made a million dollars in a day. My goal was a thousand dollars a month. This guy made a million dollars in a day. I didn't know that was even possible, but he did it. And I was like, that's my, that's my goal. I don't want to make a million dollars a day, but I want to make a million dollars. I started in the math as he had a thousand dollars course. He sold a thousand copies. What if I created a course with a thousand dollars? I sold three a day. Like if in a year, I'd make a million dollars in a year and it became so clear, so like crystal clear to me that it was possible that like it became real for me. I could taste it. I went back and afterwards I started working. I was like, I'm gonna make a million dollars. I'm gonna make it. Like it wasn't even in my radar prior to that. And I was like, I'm gonna make a million dollars, make a million dollars. The first year I went for it, I didn't make a million dollars. Next year I tried again. It's in there. I was shy of like, like 50, 60 that I didn't make it two years in a row. And I was like, John, did it. I can do this. I have to do it. And the third year, the third try, we did it. We broke it. I think we did it in $1.2 million that year. And I was like, God, we did it. But for me, the reason why I was able to do that, the reason why I'm here in front of you guys today is because John broke the four minute mile for me. We wanted to do this for inside of Click phones. I was like, what's, what's the equivalent of for us of a four minute mile? Like what can we give people to give them a goal to shoot after? Did you know that every time you create a funnel with click funnels, you are actually helping a child in Africa? Did you know that? I remember when I first got into business, I always thought like, that'd be cool to like do a charity thing. And I was always had in my head like, well, when we're successful, then we'll do a charity or when we make more money. Like, it was always this, this one thing when click funnels launched, we kind of weaved it into the DNA. Initially, it was like, Hey, whenever your funnel is built, let's donate a dollar. Like that it started from, from the very, very beginning. And it's crazy because when people understand the mission, it bonds into you at a different level than, than if you don't, you know, the companies like Tom shoe, who went and every single shoe that was sold, they, they bought a shoe and gave it to some third world country, someone who needed a shoe and like, that social mission was, was huge. And I think people want to work with companies and brands and people who aren't just about money, just about themselves, like there's more of a soul to the company than just a money thing. And so it only does it, you know, give your customers a vision of what's possible for themselves. It also helps them to connect with you, to trust you, to like you, because you're not just in it for the money. And one of the charities is village impact. I got involved with this because I love Stu and I love Amy, I love them. And I was grateful enough that they took me there and let me experience. But I love for you just kind of talk for a few minutes, just about, about where this thing came from and why you guys, why you're doing it. I'm talking to the chairman of this African community. We're talking about building a school. And then I said, how much does it cost to fund the full time salary of a teacher? And he said, it'll be about $100 a month. At the time we were, we were selling a software product for $100. And I made this connection. If I sell one more product a month, that could fund the full time salary of a teacher. And then I just kind of like expanded on that. The more money I make, the more impact I could have. And that really started to pump me up. The kickoff, like the giving process, we wanted to do something really fun. We got a huge check made up for world teacher aid. We're going to fund the next classroom for $12,000. So there you go, Stu. Thanks to you guys and your creation of funnels, we were able to raise $46,000 for world teacher aid. So you guys, in the last 12 months, she has been 78,460 funnels. Last year, you guys have built 133,737 funnels that have gone live inside of We want to give you guys hopefully the biggest check physically you've ever got before. Wow. That is crazy. You guys. We're just so grateful because every year we get invited to come back. And it's not something that we take for granted. The work that we do is in what's called IDP camps. These people were removed from their everyday life because of the violence that was happening and just moved to this rural area in the middle of nowhere. And to see their progress that's happening year over year, little by little, it's incredible. And it's having a huge ripple effect. The fact that this is woven into the business model of click funnels is humbling. And I'm so grateful to Todd and Russell and the whole ClickFunnels team. Huge credit to you guys. What I love that you're doing now is you're teaching them to be givers and seeing the value of that and getting hooked on that, you know, to me, that's the most important thing. So congratulations. Thanks again. I definitely know the fear of a lot of people about incorporating charity, especially in their events, is that, you know, people come with the wall and there's so much money in their wall. And if they spend it on charity, they may not spend it with other things you have. And I'm glad it happened to Orlando was the one that we raised the most money for the charity. And also interesting enough was the event that we made the most money in coaching sales afterward, which seemed backwards. Like you wouldn't think like those two things are tied together. I don't know, from a business decision, like you getting people to open the wall, it's for a good cause. It helps them to be more likely to open the wall. It's for you to continue to help them in the future with coaching, with your back and cells, whatever that that thing might be on the other side, because you have this opportunity to really, to really change them. We've been serving them hard for three days. And generally here is a self-amazing moment. We know without question, they are in two comma club acts. There is no chance if they do the work that they will not be a better entrepreneur. They will not do more, be more and have more in 2019. This is where we give people the ability that they want to continue this process, we get them from where they are today to where they want This is where we make the office. The whole event has been choreographed for this, right? Before people go on stage, I show them what's possible, I give them the results of their goals. Like here's the goal, you want to get a two comma club award. They see the vision and now let me show you guys the path and the process. There's three core things I need to get them to believe. The very first thing they have to believe is that this vehicle, this process is actually possible, right? So I'm telling stories to break any false beliefs, anything else is possible besides this one thing, that's step in the board. We have over 505 total people who have won the two comma club award and then 240 new ones since last time. 241 people, that's almost one person a day has said the two comma club since last time we were all here hanging out together. Is that amazing? Okay? And these numbers are going up. These aren't some like business scholars, these are your friends, these are your peers, look around, they're sitting next to you right now. Okay? This shouldn't be weird, this is happening all the time. You can't dismiss it you guys, but guess what I'm most excited about? We're out of all this stuff. I think I'm most excited for you guys on the stage next year. The next thing is people start thinking, well that's really cool for them, but like what about me? Like how is it possible for me? Some of you guys are sitting here in the audience and you're watching people come on stage and instead of saying like, what does he believe? What is he like? What are these people do? They've already done it. You're sitting back saying, no, no, no, like I believe something different. For you to be successful, it's not about conforming the rest of the world to your beliefs. So I figure out what the truth is, then conform your beliefs to that. If you want to be successful, you find people who are already successful and you model them. Okay? It's not find people who are already successful and convince them that you're right. I'm telling stories about people who are in the same situation, like who was in the audience last year sitting in the stage thinking they weren't going to be there, but this year came on stage and got the award. And then the third fault's belief I have to break is, you know, people are scared. Like, I believe this is the right process. I believe I can do it, but like, oh, my spouse or my kids don't believe me or I got a day job or all the other external things. And I've got to show them those other things aren't going to hold you back. You change your question, you change everything. Me and Todd were like almost completely broke. I was like, I'll work for free. He's like, I'll work for free. Like sweet, we can afford this.
